questions.question: Why is a single evidence source insufficient for diagnosing a multilayer outage?
options: One source is always enough
Each source shows only one layer's perspective; you need packets (L3/L4), syslog (L2/L3), app logs (L7), and routing updates to see the full causal chain
Multiple sources create confusion
Only the application log matters
questions.explanation: A packet capture shows TCP resets but not why. Syslog shows BGP withdrawal but not user impact. App logs show timeouts but not the network cause. Correlating all sources reveals the full causal chain.

questions.question: What is the root cause in incident analysis, and how does it differ from contributing factors?
options: The root cause is the last event before recovery
The root cause is the earliest event in the causal chain that is not itself caused by a prior event; contributing factors amplified the outage but did not cause it
The root cause is always a human error
Root cause and contributing factors are the same
questions.explanation: The root cause is the first domino - the earliest event with no preceding causal predecessor. Contributing factors (like missing monitoring or aggressive retries) made the outage worse but did not initiate it.

questions.question: In the payment service outage, what was the causal chain from BGP withdrawal to application failure?
options: App error -> BGP withdrawal -> TCP reset
BGP route withdrawal -> route black hole -> TCP resets -> database connection failures -> application errors -> retry storm
TCP reset -> BGP withdrawal -> app error
Retry storm -> BGP withdrawal -> black hole
questions.explanation: The causal chain flows from bottom to top: BGP withdrawal removed the route, traffic was black-holed, TCP connections reset, the database became unreachable, the application returned errors, and aggressive retries created a storm.

questions.question: What is a retry storm and why is it a contributing factor rather than the root cause?
options: A retry storm is the root cause of every outage
A retry storm is an aggressive retry pattern (e.g., 100ms timer) that amplifies an outage by generating thousands of failed requests per second, but it is a symptom, not the initiating cause
Retry storms only happen with TCP
Retry storms are harmless
questions.explanation: A retry storm with a 100ms retry timer generates 10,000 retries/second, all failing. This amplifies the outage by adding load to already-failing systems, but the root cause is whatever made the service fail in the first place.

questions.question: What sections should a production-grade postmortem document include?
options: Just a summary and who to blame
Summary, Impact, Root Cause, Causal Chain, Contributing Factors, Timeline, Action Items, and Lessons Learned
Only the timeline
Only the root cause
questions.explanation: A complete postmortem includes: Summary (what happened), Impact (who/what affected), Root Cause (first domino), Causal Chain (how it cascaded), Contributing Factors (what made it worse), Timeline (when), Action Items (what to fix), and Lessons Learned (what to remember).
